title: service-accounts — webhooks
description: service-accounts DISPATCHES no outbound webhooks. Every lifecycle event is consumed by internal listeners (notifications, activity, observability). No tenant-configurable webhook stream fires on SA state changes — that would leak inter-service credential lifecycle metadata to third parties, a compromise surface with no matching product win.

inbound_webhook_kinds.name: kms.key.rotated
inbound_webhook_kinds.description: Optional AWS EventBridge integration (Wave 1c — not shipping in Wave 1a HS256 baseline). When Wave 1c introduces RS256 keys hosted on AWS KMS, EventBridge notifies the app on KMS-driven key rotation. Received event triggers the `auth::JwtSigningKeyRotator` service which cascades to this module's `HandleSignerKidRotation` listener.
inbound_webhook_kinds.notes: Best-effort. Missing the event is not catastrophic — the auth module also runs a periodic rotation-check cron. In Wave 1a HS256 baseline, key material is Doppler-backed + rotated via ops runbook; no external webhook needed.

rationale: SA lifecycle events (created, rotated, expired, disabled) carry structured references to internal identity graph state (service_account_id, application_id, signer_kid). Exposing these to a tenant-configurable webhook allows a compromised or misconfigured tenant webhook receiver to correlate machine credentials across their tenancy — a compromise surface without a matching product win.

Tenants who legitimately need cross-system machine-credential sync should use future SCIM (deferred Wave 1c) or a dedicated integration module with contractual redaction obligations, not a general webhook.

Suspicious-activity + rotation-due notifications DO ship to tenant-adjacent recipients (Application owners), but as user-facing notifications through the notifications module — not as machine-parseable webhook payloads.
